Your letter has been forwarded to me for reply. 
Thank you for your letter of April 28, 1976, requesting 
an opinion with respect to the interpretation of Government 
Code §§87203 and 82030(a). 
No 
1 opinion will be issued 
because the questions presented do not raise a substantial 
question of interpretation under the Political Reform Act.

to 
help you resolve the matter. 
One of the main purposes of the Political Reform Act is 
to attempt to minimize potential conflicts of interest by 
public officials. 
§81001(b) of the Government Code expresses 
this sentiment precisely: 
"(b) Public officials, whether elected or 
appointed, should perform their duties in an 
impartial manner, free from bias caused by 
their own financial interests or the finan-
cial interests of persons who have supported

of any income of any trust in

A similar disclosure reauiren,e:nt aDolies to investments

The fact situation which you pose requires an examination 
of the filer's duty to inquire, in order to properly disclose. 
§81004 requires-that the -lTier use "all reClsonable diligence. 1/ 
1181004. 
All reports and s:::atements filed under 
this title shall be signed under penalty of perjury 
and verified by the filer. 
The verification shall 
state that the filer has used all reasonable dili-
gence in its preparation, and that to the best of 
his knowledge it is true and complete." 
The requirement of "all rea:::;onable diligence" in your 
specific instance could be met by attaching a letter to 
Schedule 721-C, in which the facts as they exist at the 
time of filing are disclosed; together with a short but 
definite statement of the filer's attempts to inquire. 
If you have any further questions, please contact me at 
(916) 322-6441.

CA8LE ADDRESS: "GYELAW" 
TELEX: 34·248 


Mr. Delbert Spurlock 
April 28, 1976 
Page 2 
Mrs. Mayer is a beneficiary of a trust established 
under the laws of the Bahamian government. 
We believe that 
the trust owns assets in the Untied States, but the trustee 
has never disclosed, and now refuses to disclose, what assets 
the trust owns, or the amount and nature of its annual income. 
For the past year and a half the trustee has chosen to make no 
distributions whatsoever to Mrs. Mayer. 
In 1973 the Bahamian government revoked the license 
of the trust company which acts as trustee of Mrs. Mayer's 
trust, and we are informed that the company is now operating 
in Great Britain. 
However, the trustee has proven to be very 
elusive and uncooperative and we find it very difficult to 
obtain any information from it at all. 
We have advised our client to report upon other 
income outside of the trust and to disclose the foregoing 
situation by attaching a copy of this letter to Schedule 721-C, 
the statement of Economic Interest. 
In our opinion, the particular 
requirements of Section 87203 are met by bona fide fidsclosures 
of known income and good faith attempts to quantify the extent 
and nature of the income from the trust. 
We would appreciate 
an early response so that we might file an amended Statement 
if you are unable to concur in our opinion. 
Thank you for your assistance. 
Yours very truly, 
LAW OFFICES OF 
ROBERT ERNEST GYEMANT, 
A 
s~~nal Corporation

sections.conclusion: No formal opinion will be issued because the questions do not raise a substantial question of interpretation under the Political Reform Act. The requirement of 'all reasonable diligence' under §81004 could be met by attaching a letter to Schedule 721-C disclosing facts as they exist at the time of filing, together with a statement of the filer's attempts to inquire.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a public official meet disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act when the trustee of a foreign trust refuses to disclose asset and income information, and does good faith disclosure of known income with documentation of attempted inquiry satisfy §87203?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined no formal opinion was necessary, but provided informal guidance that the 'all reasonable diligence' requirement can be satisfied by disclosing known facts and documenting good faith efforts to obtain information from an uncooperative trustee through a letter attached to Schedule 721-C.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an FPPC response letter. The document includes both the incoming request letter from the attorney (dated April 28, 1976) and the FPPC's response letter from Alfredo Flores (dated May 13, 1976). The FPPC declined formal opinion but provided substantive informal guidance. Some OCR artifacts present but document content is largely legible.

embedding.summary: An attorney requested FPPC interpretation regarding disclosure obligations for a public official who is a beneficiary of a foreign trust whose trustee refuses to disclose asset and income information. The FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion but provided informal guidance that attaching documentation of good faith inquiry attempts to the Statement of Economic Interest satisfies the reasonable diligence requirement.
